content.text: The golden path observability and security baseline is the minimum set of observability instrumentation and security controls that every agent service must implement to be deployable through the internal platform. It is called a baseline because it represents the floor, not the ceiling: all services must meet it, and teams are encouraged to build on top of it with service-specific dashboards, custom metrics, and additional security controls. The baseline is enforced by promotion gates that block deployment to production for any service that does not meet it.

The observability component of the baseline specifies that every agent service must emit structured traces using OpenTelemetry, expose a standard set of metrics (request latency, LLM token consumption, per-call cost, error rates), write structured JSON logs with trace correlation identifiers, and configure alert rules for SLO budget burn. The security component specifies mutual TLS for all service-to-service communication, JWT validation at every API entry point, secret retrieval from an approved vault rather than environment variables, egress network policies that allowlist approved external endpoints, and CI pipeline steps for static analysis and dependency vulnerability scanning.

Agent services have a broader attack surface than conventional web services because they make outbound calls to LLM APIs, tool integrations, and data sources. A service that is well-monitored but insufficiently secured may exfiltrate data through a compromised tool. A service that is well-secured but poorly observed may silently degrade quality or incur unexpected costs without the team knowing. The baseline addresses both dimensions because both are necessary for safe production operation of agent systems.

A platform-enforced baseline also reduces the cognitive load on individual teams. Without a baseline, each team must independently decide which observability signals to collect, which security controls to implement, and how to configure them. Teams with less experience in production operations will make different choices than experienced teams, creating inconsistent security postures across the fleet. A baseline with scaffolding ensures that a team generating their first agent service starts with a production-ready observability and security foundation.
Consistent observability across all services enables platform-level dashboards that aggregate health signals fleet-wide.
Security baseline enforcement prevents services with known gaps from reaching production, reducing incident exposure.
Scaffolded baseline implementation reduces the effort for new teams to achieve production readiness.
Structured logs with trace correlation identifiers enable cross-service debugging of multi-agent workflows.
Standard metric names across services enable comparative analysis — which services are the most expensive per request.
Baseline coverage by promotion gates creates an auditable record of compliance at each deployment.
Baseline requirements that are too strict can block deployment of early-stage services that are not yet production-ready, creating friction in the development workflow.
Maintaining the baseline specification and ensuring scaffolding stays current with it is ongoing platform team work.
Teams may implement baseline controls superficially to pass gates without achieving the underlying intent — metric names present but not meaningful.
The baseline represents the lowest common denominator; high-risk services require additional controls beyond the baseline that must be specified separately.
OpenTelemetry instrumentation adds latency overhead (typically 1-3ms per span) that may be relevant for latency-sensitive services.

The starter template for agent services includes baseline observability and security configuration as non-optional scaffolded components. The template generates an OpenTelemetry SDK initialization module, a Prometheus metrics registry with pre-defined metric names and labels, a structured logging configuration that injects trace and span identifiers into every log record, and a SLO alert rule template. Teams do not need to configure these from scratch; they need to supply service-specific labels (service name, team, environment) which are populated from template variables.

Security baseline components are scaffolded as middleware layers and CI pipeline steps. The mTLS configuration is provided as Kubernetes service mesh annotations (Istio or Linkerd) rather than application code, so the platform mesh enforces it without requiring each service to implement TLS termination. JWT validation is scaffolded as a middleware module that teams can configure with their service's audience and issuer. Secret access is scaffolded through a vault client module that uses Kubernetes service account identity to authenticate.

Promotion gates verify baseline compliance before a service advances from staging to production. A gate check queries the observability backend to confirm that the service has emitted traces, metrics, and logs in the past 24 hours. A security gate check queries the CI system to confirm that the most recent build passed SAST and dependency scanning steps. A configuration gate checks that the Kubernetes manifests include the required mesh annotations and that the deployment does not reference environment variables for secrets. Services that fail any gate are blocked and the owning team is notified.

attrs.alt: Golden path observability and security baseline architecture

content.text: Apply the observability baseline from the first deployment of a new agent service, not retroactively once the service is in production. Retrofitting observability into a running production service is significantly harder than starting with it. The starter template approach means that baseline instrumentation is present from day one, so teams have meaningful data from the very first production deployment.

The security baseline applies to all services without exception. Unlike observability, which can sometimes be relaxed for internal-only development services, security controls protect infrastructure that is shared across all services and teams. A single service with insufficient secret management or unvalidated JWT inputs can compromise credentials or data that affect other services. Enforce the security baseline for all environments, including staging, because staging environments often have access to production-adjacent infrastructure.

# observability/setup.py — scaffolded baseline observability module
from opentelemetry import trace
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace import TracerProvider
from opentelemetry.sdk.trace.export import BatchSpanProcessor
from opentelemetry.exporter.otlp.proto.grpc.trace_exporter import OTLPSpanExporter
from opentelemetry.instrumentation.httpx import HTTPXClientInstrumentor
from prometheus_client import Counter, Histogram, Gauge
import structlog, os

# --- Tracing setup ---
def setup_tracing(service_name: str, environment: str):
    provider = TracerProvider()
    exporter = OTLPSpanExporter(
        endpoint=os.environ["OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT"]
    )
    provider.add_span_processor(BatchSpanProcessor(exporter))
    trace.set_tracer_provider(provider)
    # Auto-instrument outbound HTTP calls (LLM API, tool calls)
    HTTPXClientInstrumentor().instrument()
    return trace.get_tracer(service_name)

# --- Standard metrics (baseline required set) ---
REQUEST_LATENCY = Histogram(
    "agent_request_duration_seconds",
    "End-to-end request latency",
    ["method", "endpoint", "status_code"]
)
LLM_TOKENS = Counter(
    "agent_llm_tokens_total",
    "LLM tokens consumed",
    ["provider", "model", "type"]  # type: prompt|completion
)
LLM_COST = Counter(
    "agent_llm_cost_usd_total",
    "LLM cost in USD",
    ["provider", "model"]
)
ERROR_COUNT = Counter(
    "agent_errors_total",
    "Error count by category",
    ["category", "endpoint"]
)

# --- Structured logging ---
def setup_logging(service_name: str, environment: str):
    structlog.configure(
        processors=[
            structlog.contextvars.merge_contextvars,  # injects trace_id, span_id
            structlog.stdlib.add_log_level,
            structlog.stdlib.PositionalArgumentsFormatter(),
            structlog.processors.TimeStamper(fmt="iso"),
            structlog.processors.JSONRenderer(),
        ]
    )
    return structlog.get_logger().bind(service=service_name, env=environment)

# k8s/deployment.yaml — security baseline annotations and env references
apiVersion: apps/v1
kind: Deployment
metadata:
  name: {{ service_name }}
  annotations:
    # mTLS enforced by service mesh (Istio)
    sidecar.istio.io/inject: "true"
spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        traffic.sidecar.istio.io/excludeOutboundIPRanges: ""
    spec:
      serviceAccountName: {{ service_name }}-sa  # for Vault auth
      containers:
        - name: {{ service_name }}
          # Secrets from Vault via init container or CSI driver — NOT env vars
          env:
            - name: LLM_API_KEY
              valueFrom:
                secretKeyRef:
                  name: {{ service_name }}-secrets  # populated by Vault agent
                  key: llm-api-key
            - name: OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT
              value: "http://otel-collector.platform.svc:4317"
          # Security context
          securityContext:
            runAsNonRoot: true
            readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
            allowPrivilegeEscalation: false

A new data-summarization agent service is scaffolded from the starter template. The template generates the observability setup module with the five required metric instruments, the structured logging configuration, and Kubernetes deployment annotations for Istio mTLS injection. The team focuses on implementing their business logic — the summarization prompt, the document chunking logic, the result formatting — while the observability and security scaffolding is already in place. On their first deployment to staging, Grafana immediately shows request latency histograms and token consumption counters without any additional configuration work.

During the promotion gate check for the service's first production deployment, the security gate detects that the Kubernetes deployment manifest references an environment variable for the LLM API key directly rather than through the Vault-backed secret. The gate blocks promotion and surfaces the finding to the team. The team updates the manifest to use the Vault-backed secretKeyRef pattern (which was in the scaffold but was accidentally overwritten during development), the gate check passes, and the promotion proceeds.

Six months after initial deployment, the platform team adds response content filtering as a new baseline requirement for agent services that process user-submitted inputs. The requirement is added to the golden path specification, and a new promotion gate check enforces it. The platform team provides a shared library module implementing the filtering requirement and updates the starter template to include it. Existing services receive automated upgrade PRs via Renovate bot that add the filtering middleware. Teams review and merge the PRs; services that do not merge within the 90-day SLA receive escalation notifications.

Netflix's Paved Road program (described in their engineering blog) and Spotify's Backstage golden path model both implement this pattern: a defined set of non-negotiable infrastructure requirements that every service must meet, scaffolded into project templates so that teams do not need to configure them from scratch, and enforced by CI gates so that requirements cannot be accidentally omitted in production deployments. Netflix specifically describes how their paved road includes observability, security scanning, and secrets management as non-optional components, with their internal tooling checking compliance at deployment time. Teams that want to deviate from the paved road must file an exception with the platform team and accept the associated support implications.
Defining a baseline that is too broad or too detailed, creating compliance overhead that teams work around rather than genuinely implementing.
Scaffolding baseline components as optional or easily removable, so teams delete them when they cause initial friction.
Not keeping the baseline specification and scaffolding in sync — teams using old templates generate services missing new baseline requirements.
Checking only for the presence of metric instruments, not for whether they are actually populated with meaningful data.
Treating the baseline as a static checklist rather than a living standard that evolves with the threat landscape and operational learnings.
Failing to provide clear remediation guidance when a gate check fails, leaving teams to figure out how to fix the violation on their own.
Document each baseline requirement with its security or operational rationale; teams comply more consistently when they understand the reason.
Keep the baseline small enough to be achievable without significant effort by a team focused on business logic; move nice-to-haves to a recommended-but-optional tier.
Version the baseline specification and communicate changes with advance notice and migration support.
Test baseline gate checks regularly against known-passing and known-failing service configurations to detect regressions in gate logic.
Provide a local compliance checker that teams can run during development before submitting for promotion, so they discover violations early.
Collect and publish fleet-wide baseline compliance metrics to create positive social pressure toward compliance.

OpenTelemetry trace sampling is the primary performance lever for observability at scale. Sampling 100% of traces for a high-throughput service produces volumes that overwhelm trace storage and increase per-request latency from span serialization. Configure tail-based sampling in the OTel collector to capture 100% of error traces and slow traces (above the p99 latency threshold) while sampling a configurable fraction of successful requests within latency targets. This preserves the high-value traces for debugging while managing volume.

Security gate checks at promotion time can become a bottleneck if they require real-time queries to multiple external systems. Design gate checks to query cached compliance state rather than performing live checks for every promotion request. The CI system updates cached state after each build; the promotion gate reads the cache. This design keeps promotion fast even when the fleet is large, while ensuring that the cached state reflects the most recent build.

The security baseline must itself be hardened against bypass. Promotion gates that can be disabled by team members with sufficient repository permissions defeat the purpose of baseline enforcement. Implement gate checks as external validations run by the platform CI system, not as steps in the team's own CI pipeline that can be removed or skipped. The platform CI system should have authority over promotion decisions independent of the team's pipeline, with audit logs of every gate check result.

Reliability of the observability baseline is important because services that cannot emit telemetry are flying blind in production. Include circuit breakers in the OTel exporter configuration so that if the trace collector becomes unavailable, the service continues operating rather than failing. Log to both the structured pipeline and to stderr as a fallback so that logs are never completely lost even if the log pipeline has issues. Alert on telemetry pipeline failures separately from application SLO alerts.
The golden path baseline defines the minimum observability and security requirements for every agent service, enforced by promotion gates.
Scaffolding baseline components into the starter template ensures new services start compliant without additional setup work.
Observability baseline: traces (OTel), standard metrics (latency, tokens, cost), structured logs with trace IDs, SLO alerts.
Security baseline: mTLS, JWT validation, Vault-backed secrets, egress allowlisting, SAST and dependency scanning in CI.
Promotion gates must be external to team pipelines to prevent bypass; audit logs of gate results are compliance evidence.
The baseline is a living standard that evolves with the threat landscape — version it, communicate changes, and provide migration support.
